steps.title: Name the rule
steps.content: Two rules apply here. First, "its" is the possessive pronoun (no apostrophe), while "it's" is the contraction of "it is." Second, an apostrophe is used for possession ("wings'") only when another noun follows it; a bare "wings'" at the end of a phrase has nothing to own. The blank needs a possessive pronoun plus a plain plural noun — the butterfly possesses its wings.

steps.title: Test each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — its wings — correct possessive pronoun "its" followed by the plural noun "wings."
  • Choice B — its wings' — adds a stray possessive apostrophe after "wings" with no following noun to be possessed.
  • Choice C — it's wing's — uses the contraction "it is" ("it is wing's in Brazil" is nonsense) and a singular possessive with no following noun.
  • Choice D — it's wings' — same contraction error plus a stray possessive apostrophe on "wings."
That is the correct answer.
